item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I'd rather not have the 423 crosslink, if that's alright — I have some writing in the works involving them both, and I still want it to be plausible that the two would interact while under Foundation control. The 2976 link seems perfect to me, both in-universe and out, so full approval for that :)

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. So with how infectious this thing is, the last thing you want it to do is infect other document based entities, like SCP-423 or SCP-2976. Best to keep those apart from one another and in different sites. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-3317 is currently contained in three separate documents, two physical and one digital. For the reasons of security, the nature or location of these documents can not be divulged without permission from at least three senior researchers, and such requests will be handled on a case-by-case basis.

To this:

SCP-3317 is currently contained in three separate documents, two physical and one digital. For the reasons of security, the nature or location of these documents can not be divulged without permission from at least three senior researchers, and such requests will be handled on a case-by-case basis. At no point may any additional text-based anomalies, including SCP-423 or SCP-2976, be stored at the same location as these documents.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see your point there, but I can definitely imagine some researchers gathered around a water cooler talking about the scips they're assigned to. There's no harm in talking to staff about other low-risk entities and objects, and it probably does wonders for morale, but this is something that you don't want people accidentally writing about without fully understanding its effects — while it may be common sense, I think it's still worth emphasising.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that it's intentionally misreading Marx, but without a comment to the effect, it seems indistinguishable from the author misreading Marx. The only change I would suggest is in the cross-contamination attempts, that they might also have attempted to introduce some essays written in opposition to that from a Marxist perspective, in the hopes that, even if they didn't contradict its views, they could get it to calm down?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"are strictly forbidden from mentioning the anomaly, even verbally, outside of the work environment"
Does this part need to be said? I feel like this is standard procedure with almost all SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last line is a real stinger - both absurd and completely reasonable. I appreciate stuff like that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fair. I only found out about 2553 after I wrote this, and it would honestly have been helpful to read it before; it covers the concept much more competently than I ever could have. I'd like to think mine is sufficiently different to be viable as an article, but I can understand how it wouldn't appeal - I'm still finding my feet when it comes to writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't do it for me, and a bit too close to SCP-2553 without actually being compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fun to think of what would happen if this was exposed to a book like 1984. Perhaps the entity would change its mind…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the "lack of foresight" bit, all it would really take is someone writing up a list of all occupied slots in the same directory as a link to a collection of political works. It's an error made by incompetence, sure, but not really unreasonable.

As for the other comments, I get what you mean, and will try to do something better for my next scip attempt :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never read "The Communist Manifesto" so I can't provide input re: accuracy to Marxism. However, I can say that this was not the most entertaining of directions to take this narrative.

The concept is basically SCP-423 crossed with SCP-140. But the only thing that happens when this weird, self-modifying narrative entity encounters "The Communist Manifesto" is, it becomes a 14-year-old who listens to Rage Against the Machine too much.

It's a self-spreading, self-modifying, sapient being whose mind would be totally alien to any human. Its behavior shouldn't be predictable. And the Foundation leaving a copy of one of the most influential books in human history idly next to it demonstrates a lack of foresight on their part. Overall, I'm neutral-voting because it's competently executed but narratively unengaging. Sorry, boss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, 30 is young. But I think works like The Critique of the Gotha Program in 1875 (when he was 57) are fairly well indicative that he hadn't softened on the dictatorship of the proletariat or on revolutionary not evolutionary change (although I think in Capital he allows that some societies with strong democratic traditions would be better suited for political evolution towards socialism rather than destructive revolution; so maybe that is what he's referring to).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the older Marx advocated bloodless revolution and disowned violent "Marxist" revolutionary groups, a young and passionate Marx wrote the Communist Manifesto.

Um no. That's bullshit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think q 17 and Flame Shirt are also ignoring another important aspect here: There's a reason militant Leninism embraced the work as something like a holy text.

While the older Marx advocated bloodless revolution and disowned violent "Marxist" revolutionary groups, a young and passionate Marx wrote the Communist Manifesto. There's a subtle anger woven into the work that makes it easy to interpret as "The evil overlords are responsible for all of your suffering and have made you slaves. Unite, destroy them and their corrupt society, and you can create a new world order from the ashes."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to me that this entity is doing exactly what Stalin, Lenin, Mao, Castro etc did. Taking the philosophy and twisting it (in some cases already having started with a twisted version from previous iterations) to suit their own power hungry, paranoid or delusional goals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, dude, dude, it's not the quotes that are correct that we're criticising, it's the stuff you've put alongside the quotes that make it unbelievable. It reads like you've just skimmed the manifesto for choice quotes, then made up the rest (and incorrectly at that, unfortunately).

Like I said in chat, I feel like if the article actually played with the inaccuracies, it could be quite fun. But as the article is right now, it's seems like the author didn't understand the source material, not the SCP itself. The comments here saying otherwise just read like ex post facto statements trying to say that the article says stuff that it doesn't right now.

The conversation we had that I showed above pretty much sums it up. To quote from of my favourite critics of historical films, "If you don't give a damn about the source material, why the hell should I care about what you've made?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Let the ruling classes tremble at a Communistic revolution."

"The proletarians have nothing to lose but their chains."

"In one word, you reproach us with intending to do away with your property."

"The ruling ideas of each age have ever been the ideas of its ruling class."

"like the sorcerer, who is no longer able to control the powers of the nether world."

Although, admittedly, that last one was mutilated quite a bit. As I said before, and will probably say again, this thing picks and chooses individual words, phrases, and fragments of ideas. This thing was meant to be a haphazard, brutally mashed together amalgamation of various pieces of literature, not a direct parody of Communism. Which is why "Libre la Livres" is French, not Russian or German.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read TCM, it sounds nothing like this at all. It isn't just "misusing political goals", it's got literally no relation to what communist literature sounds like. The only things I can see are "glorious" and the nothing-to-lose quote – and "glorious" isn't actually in the Manifesto.

To be frank, it's terrible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I was actually going for was the result of applying certain parts of Marxist ideals to an already invasive and expanding concept. The result (which is, admittedly, more similar to Stalinism) is what you see there: the furthering of their own means via misuse of political goals.

As seen in the Ayn Rand bit, this Scip picks and chooses which sections of text it wishes to combine with.

EDIT: Also, what I said there was that I hadn't read the entire thing, not that I didn't understand the core concepts. I know that Marxism is different from Stalinism, and did actually do research before posting this; hence the actual Manifesto quotes in the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People reading and misapplying the communist manifesto is what got us stalinism. Why would a sentient meme (or whatever) do a better job of it? Especially considering it clearly wasn't meant to be applied to sentient literature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty ridiculous that this thing is talking about empire-building and so on, considering the Communist Manifesto was written just after the Scramble for Africa. Imperialism was exactly the kind of thing this book was against.

This thing reads far more like a lazy parody of Stalinism, which the Communist Manifesto pre-dates by half a century.

Edit:

21:21 FlameShirt MaliceAforethought: Have you like, ever read the communist manifesto?
21:21 MaliceAforethought FlameShirt No, why?
21:22 MaliceAforethought FlameShirt Well, not all of it

This makes me both :| and :<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If Communist texts increased the rate of expansion, it would make sense that isolationist texts would reduce it. They didn't, but it would have made sense.

As for the Ayn Rand bit, it's a contrasting ideology that may have 'neutralised' the Communist ideas. I did say it was misguided.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's pretty neat. I'll upvote for Ayn Rand shenanigans

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would they attempt to neutralize it by cross contaminating it more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was honestly hoping for some kind of format screw, where we might find different versions of the article with different things appended, but I got what you were going for here, and I think it works regardless. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keyboard shortcuts suck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The memetic narrative thing has been done already, but this brings a fresh and hilarious take to the concept without being gimmicky. My only critique would be that the Atlas Shrugged stuff is just a little too on the nose. But only just a little. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hilarity of a sapient Marxist narrative is hillarious enough that I was already going to +1 this, but if it hadn't been,

Research into SCP-3317's possible sentience or status as a sovereign state is ongoing.

would have done it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a bit ambivalent about this one in chat, but the stuff about Atlas Shrugged at the end has made it a whole lot better and given it an entirely different tone. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I stuck them in for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, it should be blackboxed but I no longer have laptop access and it's near impossible to do on mobile. I'll fix it when I can, or get someome else to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming the ##/##/#### still in there is a mistake, but it can be hard to tell with ones like these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah dang, missed that. Alright, Euclid it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once affected, the secondary text will be replaced with a full copy of SCP-3317, and the original has been rendered unrecoverable in all cases so far.

It does destroy the original documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love to see this up, very weird interesting thing. It does evolve the more information it gets, and it can get information pretty easily, but if it doesn't harm the original documentation, is it really Euclid? At that point, a containment breach poses zero threat, unless things can access its IP address and find the Foundation, right?

Anyways, love it lots. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everybody who reviewed this in chat! It's come a long way since its first iteration, and I'm reasonably happy with how it's turned out.

And yes, those are real Marx quotes embedded in the addendum. Isn't that something?

Edit regarding communism, pseudo-communism, and variants thereof: Okay, so, it's been a little while since I posted this. I was unnecessarily defensive of myself when people criticised this (what with it being my first SCP and all), and I think it's time to explain myself properly.

This is not what I think communism is. I think communism is a pretty good ideology, to be honest (though it has problems like everything else), and this article is not me trying to slander either it, or the authors of the Communist Manifesto. I won't get into a discussion of communism's merits here (it's hardly the correct place), but you'll have to take my word for it.

This article is supposed to be a parody2, in a sense — it's me attempting to replicate the willful misinterpretation of communist ideals to suit an individual's own desires. A parody of the malicious parodies of communism, if you will. I completely failed to express that in a concise manner, and I only have myself to blame for that. Apologies to Flameshirt, TheShrike, q 17 and everyone else who I dragged into an unnecessary debate about what was intended as a harmless dig at misunderstanding: and no, the irony is not lost on me.
